entry.title: Digital Signature – Signature Validation Error Using SSFW_KRN_SIGN and STRUST Certificate
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are working on a Malaysia e-Invoice digital signature implementation in SAP ECC 6.0.

We have successfully imported our signing certificate into STRUST and are currently generating the digital signature using Function Module SSFW_KRN_SIGN.

However, when the signed XML is submitted to the portal, we receive a Signature Validation Error.

  • Is SSFW_KRN_SIGN the recommended function module for generating XML digital signatures in ECC 6.0, or should another FM/class be used?
  • For the signer information structure (SSFINFO), what values should be passed for:
    • ID (SSFID)
    • PROFILE (SSFPROF)
  • How can we verify that the certificate configured in STRUST is correctly being used during the signing process?

    Currently, the signature is being generated successfully, but the external portal reports a signature validation failure.

Any guidance or details would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 07 - Authorization annotations
Released API DDLS Was Deleted ATC Error in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Despite Object No Longer
entry.content:
Hello,

I am working in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and I am facing compatibility ATC errors during ATC checks.

The following ATC errors are raised:

  • Released API DDLS ZDD_SD_TEST was deleted

  • Released API DDLS ZDD_SD_OPENORDERITEM was deleted

  • Released API DDLS ZDD_SD_DELIVERY was deleted

Error example:

Changes in ZSD (DEVC), compared to version 100 (CG4), found by component BC-SRV-APS-EXT-REL:
Released API DDLS ZDD_SD_TEST was deleted.

Background:

  • These CDS views were previously released as APIs (C1 contract).

  • The CDS views no longer exist in the system.

  • When searching in ADT (Ctrl+Shift+A), the objects cannot be found.

  • In the "Manage API Snapshots" application, I cannot find these CDS views in the current SAP Default snapshot.

  • However, the compatibility ATC check still reports them as deleted APIs.

I found SAP KBA 3442779 which explains that released APIs may remain in historical API snapshots and can continue to trigger compatibility checks even after deletion.

My questions are:

  1. How can I identify the exact snapshot that still contains these deleted CDS views?

  2. If the objects no longer exist and were never delivered to customers, what is the recommended way to remove these compatibility findings?

  3. Is setting the default snapshot to "Not Check Relevant" the only solution, or is there another recommended approach?

  4. Has anyone experienced a similar issue with deleted released CDS views in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: How to create a custom tile of a custom analytical query for specific Bookmark
entry.content:
Hello SAP Folks,

I have a scenario such that i want to create a custom tile for each bookmarks that i have made in a custom analytical query. 

For example - Query "ZTRIP_DETAILS" have bookmarks saved:-
1. " Billed Trips"
2. " Unbilled Trips"

So I want to Create custom insight tiles of both the bookmarks in s4hana private cloud such that if user for example search for " Billed Trips - Trip Details" then only billed bookmark should open as an application.

Note:- My SAP Version is 2022 and i am working in s4hana private cloud.

Could you guys please help me build tile for each bookmarks of the query?
Also i have attached an image for your reference.
Thank you in advance.

Thanks
Vishal 

SAP HANA, platform edition

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 07 - Authorization annotations
SAP Secure Login Client on macOS – plans for Apple Silicon native support?
entry.content:
Hello,

we are currently using the SAP Secure Login Client on macOS, and we’ve noticed that the latest available version is still built only for the Intel (x86_64) architecture.

At the moment, this is not an immediate issue, as macOS (currently version 26) still supports Rosetta 2, and Apple has indicated that Rosetta will remain available in macOS 27. However, based on Apple’s roadmap, Rosetta 2 support is expected to be discontinued around macOS 28, which would effectively prevent Intel-only applications from running on Apple Silicon devices.

Given that the transition to Apple Silicon is already well advanced across enterprise environments, this raises a concern for the future usability of the Secure Login Client on macOS.

My questions are:

  • Are there any plans to release a native Apple Silicon (ARM) version of the SAP Secure Login Client for macOS?
  • Is there an official roadmap or timeline for this transition?
  • Alternatively, is there a recommended replacement or different approach for secure authentication on macOS in SAP environments once Rosetta 2 is no longer available?

Thank you in advance for any insights.

entry.title: Skill replication to FSM
entry.content:
Replication Configuration Settings for FSM

  • Configure the FSM replication settings in the SAP IMG by navigating to: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Integration. This activity sets the RFC destination, Account/Company IDs, Client Version, and the REST API paths used to communicate with the FSM cloud tenant.

Single Company Setup

  • For a single-company deployment, provide the FSM Account ID and Company ID in the same IMG node: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Integration. These values uniquely identify your FSM tenant and ensure that replicated objects are routed to the correct company context.

 

Multiple Company Setup

  • When multiple SAP company codes need to replicate data to different FSM companies, define the company-to-company mapping in: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Multiple Company Replication. Each entry maps an S/4HANA company code to its corresponding FSM company, allowing fine-grained control over which data is shared with which FSM tenant.

Scale Replication

  • Scale replication is a ‘manual’ activity — unlike Qualification replication, it does not trigger automatically when a Scale is created or changed. You must explicitly execute the replication program described below.
  • Before proceeding, verify that all prerequisite configuration steps have been completed. Refer to the ‘Configure Replication of Scales’ IMG activity for the full checklist (RFC destination, API paths, Account/Company IDs).Configure Replication of Scales

Step 1: Create Scales in S/4HANA

  • Open the IMG activity ‘Maintain Scales’ by navigating to: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Basic Functions → Qualifications → Maintain Scales. This is the starting point for defining all proficiency scales that will later be replicated to FSM as ‘Levels’ within a skill category.

  • Press ‘New Entries’ and provide a unique Scale ID (e.g., 51) and a descriptive Scale Name (e.g., Hydraulic_Technician). Save the entry. The system will then prompt you to define the scale type — choose ‘Quality Scale’ (as opposed to Quantity Scale) because FSM skills use named proficiency levels rather than numeric ranges.

  • Select the ‘Characteristic Values’ node in the dialog structure on the left. Add one row per proficiency level, assigning a sequential ID and a meaningful name. For example, for the Hydraulic_Technician scale: ID 1 = Basic, ID 2 = Advanced, ID 3 = Experienced. These names will appear verbatim as skill levels inside FSM.

  • Optionally, select the ‘Proficiency descriptions’ node to add longer descriptions for each level (e.g., “Awareness; requires supervision” for the Basic level). These descriptions provide additional context to dispatchers when matching technician skills to service order requirements.
  • Create a second Scale to cover a different skill category. For example, create ‘Safety_Certifications’ (ID 52) as a Quality Scale with two characteristic values: ID 1 = Pass, ID 2 = Fail. This binary scale is appropriate for certification checks such as safety training or compliance tests.

 

Step 2: Replicate Scales to FSM

  • Once the scales are created and saved, replicate them to FSM by executing the IMG activity ‘Replicate Scales’ found at: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Master Data Replication → Replicate Scales. This launches a selection screen where you specify which scales to send.

 

  • Enter one or more Scale IDs in the ‘Scales ID (Multiple)’ field or use the F4 value help to select from the list. Ensure the ‘Test Mode’ checkbox is —unchecked— so the replication is executed for real. Click the Execute button (F8) to start the process.

 

  • The system processes the replication synchronously and immediately displays an execution log. Green information messages confirm success; red error messages indicate failures that need to be investigated. Key log messages to look for include: “Scale Found”, “Relevant scale data found!”, “RFC destination is maintained”, “Account and company determination is successful”, and “Scale Replications started”.

  • After successful replication, open the FSM application and navigate to Settings and Configuration → Master Data → Scales. Both replicated scales (e.g., Hydraulic_Technician and Safety_Certifications) should now be visible in the list, along with their proficiency levels on the right-hand panel.

 

Deleting Scales in FSM

  • To remove all scales from FSM, first delete or deactivate the relevant Scale entries in S/4HANA, then re-run the same ‘Replicate Scales’ IMG activity. This time, leave the Scale ID field empty and check the ‘Delete Scale/Proficiency’ checkbox before executing. This instructs the program to clear all existing scale data in FSM.

    After the deletion run is completed, you can re-run the replication (without the checkbox and with specific Scale IDs) to reload only the desired scales into FSM. This is the recommended approach for refreshing scale data after bulk changes.

Error Log and Troubleshooting — Scales

  • Scale replication communicates with FSM asynchronously via HTTP. If an HTTP-level error occurs (e.g., authentication failure, network timeout, or malformed payload), it will not appear in the ABAP short dump but can be traced in the AIF (Application Interface Framework) error log. Navigate to transaction ‘AIF’ or use the Monitoring and Error Handling screen, filtering on Namespace ‘/FSM’ and Interface Name ‘SCALE_OUT’.

In the AIF error log, select a failed message from the left-hand panel. The right-hand panel displays the full JSON payload that was sent to FSM, along with detailed error information. Common issues include: invalid Scale IDs, missing RFC destination configuration, incorrect Account/Company mapping, or FSM API rate limiting. After fixing the root cause, you can restart failed messages directly from the AIF screen.

Qualification / Skill Replication

  • Qualification (also referred to as ‘Skill’ in FSM) replication is triggered both automatically (real-time, whenever a Qualification Group or Qualification is created or changed in S/4HANA) and manually (on demand via transaction DRFOUT). This dual-mode approach ensures the FSM skill catalog stays in sync with the S/4HANA qualification catalog without requiring scheduled batch jobs.
  • Before creating qualifications, verify that all prerequisite configuration steps have been completed. Refer to the IMG activity ‘Configure Replication of Qualifications’ for the full checklist, including the Outbound Implementation settings and DRF (Data Replication Framework) configuration.Configure Replication of Qualifications

 

Step 1: Create a Qualification Group

  • A Qualification Group acts as a category or container for related individual qualifications. Create a new group using the IMG activity: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Basic Functions → Qualifications → Edit Qualifications Catalog, or by calling transaction ‘OOQA’ directly. The Qualifications Catalog is structured as a tree; groups are the parent nodes.

  • In the Qualifications Catalog screen, click the ‘Create’ button and choose ‘Qualification group’ from the popup. Enter a short ID (e.g., QK prefix is auto-assigned), a descriptive name (e.g., ‘Hydraulic Systems’), a validity period, and optionally a longer description. In the ‘Scale’ dropdown, select the scale you created earlier (e.g., 51 Hydraulic_Technician). This links the proficiency levels to all qualifications within this group.

  • After saving the Qualification Group, switch to the ‘Proficiencies’ tab in the dialog. The proficiency levels are automatically inherited from the selected scale (e.g., 1 = Basic, 2 = Advanced, 3 = Experienced). Select the radio button next to the level that represents the ‘default’ required proficiency — this default is used when a service order does not specify an explicit proficiency requirement.

 

Step 2: Create Individual Qualifications (Skills)

  • Individual qualifications represent specific skills or competencies (e.g., ‘Component maintenance’, ‘Hydraulic System design’). To create one, place the cursor on the parent Qualification Group in the catalog tree and press ‘Create’. In the popup, choose ‘Qualification’ (not ‘Qualification group’). Fill in a name, description, and confirm the inherited scale.

  • Repeat this process to build a complete skill catalog. For example, under the ‘Hydraulic Systems’ group you might add: Hose & Fitting Assembly, Hydraulic System design, Pressure testing & Fault finding, and Component maintenance. Create additional groups as needed (e.g., ‘Safety Certifications’ linked to scale 52) with their own qualifications (e.g., Forklift/MEWP license, Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)). The resulting tree structure mirrors the skill hierarchy that will be visible in FSM.

  • Qualification replication to FSM is triggered automatically upon save. Once replication completes successfully, open the FSM application and navigate to Master Data → Skills. You will see the qualification groups mapped as ‘skill categories’ and the individual qualifications mapped as ‘skills’, each with their assigned scale levels.

 

Manual Replication (On Demand)

  • While automatic replication handles most day-to-day synchronization, you may need to trigger replication manually — for example after a bulk import, a system migration, or to force-resend a specific qualification. Use transaction ‘DRFOUT’ (Execute Data Replication) for this purpose.
  • Open transaction DRFOUT. Set the ‘Replication Model’ to ‘FSM_INT’ (RM for BO replication to FSM) and the ‘Outbound Implementation’ to ‘ORG_REPL’. Set the ‘Replication Mode’ to ‘Manual’ and tick the ‘Manual Replication Filter Criteria Available’ checkbox. Then click the ‘Manual Replication Filter Criteria’ button at the top of the screen.

  • In the filter selection screen, expand the ‘Filter for org data’ node. Enter the Qualification Group ID or Qualification ID in the ‘Object ID’ field (use F4 help to browse). Set the ‘Object type’ accordingly (e.g., ‘OK’ for Qualification Group, ‘Q’ for individual Qualification). Save the filter, return to the previous screen, and click ‘Execute’ (F8).

  • The system displays a replication log screen. Green messages indicate that the qualification data was successfully packaged and sent to FSM. If errors appear, check the AIF error log (see ‘Error Log and Troubleshooting’ section below).

 

Deleting a Qualification Group or Qualification in FSM

  • Deletions and changes are handled in real time. When you delete or change a Qualification Group or individual Qualification in S/4HANA (via OOQA or the IMG activity), the change event is immediately captured by the Data Replication Framework and sent to FSM. There is no need to manually trigger a replication after a deletion. If an error occurs during the sync, investigate it in the AIF error log described below.

Error Log and Troubleshooting — Qualifications

  • Qualification replication communicates with FSM asynchronously via HTTP. If an error occurs at the HTTP transport layer, it will be recorded in the AIF error log. Navigate to AIF Monitoring and Error Handling, and filter on Namespace ‘/FSM’ and Interface Name ‘QUAL_OUT’ to see qualification-specific messages. Select the date range that covers when the replication was triggered.

  • Expand a failed message in the left panel to see its individual transfer records. Select a record to view the JSON payload and the specific error in the right panel. The payload follows the ‘PAYLOAD:/AIF/JSON_LINE_S’ structure, showing the qualification tree sent to FSM. After diagnosing the issue (e.g., missing skill category mapping, FSM authentication error, or invalid external ID), fix the root cause and restart the failed message from the AIF screen.

Skill Assignment to Employees

Step 1: Create the Employee Master Record

  • Employee records in S/4HANA are created and maintained in HR Master Data transaction ‘PA30’ (Maintain HR Master Data). Open PA30, leave the Personnel Number field blank, select the ‘Actions’ infotype from the list, and press ‘Create’ to start a new hiring action.

In the ‘Create Actions’ screen, set the Action Type (e.g., 01 Hiring), Reason for Action, and the Start Date. Proceed through the infotype screens to provide: Personal Data (name, birthdate, gender, language, nationality), Organizational Assignment (company code, personnel area, employee group/subgroup, position, org unit), and Communication details (email, SAP user ID link if applicable). Save each infotype screen to create the complete employee record.

  • If the employee will log on to the SAP system, link the employee record to their SAP user ID using the ‘Communication’ infotype (IT0105, subtype 0001 SY-BNAME). This connection is required for certain self-service and approval workflows.

  • After the employee record is saved, run program ‘/SHCM/RH_SYNC_BUPA_EMPL_SINGLE’ with the new Personnel Number. This program creates or updates the Business Partner (BP) record in S/4HANA that corresponds to this employee. The BP record is the object that is ultimately replicated to FSM as a ‘Person’ (technician).

  • You can verify that the BP was created successfully by checking the CDS view ‘I_BUSINESSUSER’. Search for the employee’s last name or Personnel Number. The result should show one row with the BP GUID, Business Partner number, and the linked SAP user name, confirming the employee-BP linkage is intact.

Step 2: Assign Skills (Qualifications) to the Employee

  • Use transaction ‘PPPM’ (Maintain Qualifications Profile) to assign qualifications to the employee. Open PPPM and switch to the ‘Person’ search tab. Search by name or Personnel Number. Select the employee from the ‘Hit List’ at the bottom-left and open the record in Change mode. Navigate to the ‘Qualifications’ tab.

  • Click ‘Create’ to add a new qualification entry. In the dialog, search for the qualification either by ‘Description’ (e.g., ‘Forklift / MEWP license’) or by ‘Qualification group’ (e.g., ‘Safety Certifications’). Once a qualification is selected, the ‘Proficiency’ dropdown becomes active — choose the appropriate level (e.g., Pass). Set the validity dates and save. Repeat for each skill the employee holds. The assigned qualifications will be automatically replicated to FSM as part of the person’s skill profile.

Step 3: Replicate the Business Partner (Employee) to FSM

  • While qualification changes trigger automatic replication, the initial BP replication must be triggered manually. Open transaction ‘DRFOUT’ and set: Replication Model = FSM_INT, Outbound Implementation = 986_3 (or the BP-specific implementation in your system). Set Replication Mode to ‘Manual’ and click ‘Manual Replication Filter Criteria’.

  • In the filter screen, enter the Business Partner number in the ‘Business Partner’ field (obtained from the /SHCM/RH_SYNC_BUPA_EMPL_SINGLE run or from I_BUSINESSUSER). Save the filter, return to the main screen, and execute. After successful replication, open FSM and navigate to Master Data → People. The employee should appear as an active person entry with their skills visible under the ‘Skills’ tab of their People record.

 

 

Skill Assignment to Service Orders

  • Skill requirements can be attached to a Service Order to indicate which technician competencies are needed for the job. Open the relevant Service Order in the S/4HANA Service application, navigate to the ‘Qualification Requirements’ tab on the Service Order Header, and click ‘Edit’. Assign newly created skills to Service Order Header and Item in ‘Qualification Requirements’ tab, save and ‘Release’ the Service Order.FSM will use these skill requirements during scheduling to match the order with qualified technicians.

Skills assignment at header level

 

Skills assignment at item level

 

Skills as shown in FSM activity:

entry.title: Java System Startup Issue: Application Server Unable to Connect to Message Server
entry.content:
Overview

This blog post describes a production issue where a SAP Java application server failed to start because it could not connect to the Message Server. The root cause was an SSL/PSE certificate misconfiguration on the SCS instance, which prevented the HTTPS port from activating. Combined with a security hardening change that had also disabled the HTTP port, the application server had no available port to reach the Message Server on.

 

Symptom

After starting the SCS instance, the Java Application Server (AS Java) failed to come up. Reviewing the AS Java startup logs (std_server*.log or dev_server*.log) revealed the following error:

This error indicates the AS Java was attempting to discover the Message Server but found neither the HTTP nor HTTPS port to be reachable.

 

Investigation

Step 1 — Checking dev_ms logs

The dev_ms log (located at /usr/sap/<SID>/SCS<NR>/work/dev_ms) showed that the Message Server HTTPS port had failed to activate due to an SSL/PSE error

The key error is SSSLERR_PSE_MISSING_PIN — the SSL PSE file (SAPSSLS.pse) used by the SCS instance for server-side HTTPS was either corrupt, had a mismatched PIN, or its certificate had not been properly signed and imported.

Step 2 — Confirming HTTP port was also disabled

Checking the Message Server profile (DEFAULT.PFL or instance profile of SCS) confirmed the HTTP port had been intentionally disabled as part of a security hardening measure recommended by an Onapsis vulnerability assessment

With #ms/server_port_0 , the plain HTTP Message Server port was deactivated. The only available port for external AS Java connectivity was HTTPS. Since HTTPS also failed to activate (due to the PSE issue), the Message Server was effectively unreachable from the application server perspective.

Root Cause

The root cause was a broken SSL/PSE configuration on the SCS instance. The SAPSSLS.pse file — which holds the server certificate used by the Message Server for HTTPS — was either:

  • Not properly signed by a trusted CA (or self-signed certificate not re-signed after a system change),
  • Referencing a PIN/credential that no longer matched, or
  • Corrupted / not updated after a certificate renewal or system copy.

As a result, the HTTPS port on the Message Server could not initialize. Since the HTTP port was simultaneously disabled (Onapsis hardening), there was no port available for the AS Java to communicate with the SCS Message Server, causing the startup to fail entirely.

Resolution:

The fix involved re-creating the PSE file since the Password we had was not working and then re-performing the SCS HTTPS SSL certificate signing process by Re-sign the certificate using the appropriate CA then Import the signed certificate back into the PSE (SAPSSLS.pse) for the SCS instance. Restart the SCS instance and monitor the dev_ms log to confirm the HTTPS port activates successfully. Restarted the applcation server and now it was able to connect to message server.

Key Takeaways

Always verify PSE/SSL certificates on SCS after any certificate renewal, system copy, or credential rotation.

When disabling the HTTP port on the Message Server (e.g., per Onapsis or security hardening guidelines), ensure the HTTPS port is fully functional before the change goes live — otherwise you lose all AS Java connectivity. Port activation can be verified either from dev_ms logs or we can even use https://<sapscs/ascs servername>:<httpsport>/msgserver/commands and if this url opening with HTTPS that mean SSL is successfully activated.

entry.title: Post-SAP Sapphire briefing SAP Business AI Platform – BUILD
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire introduced a bold vision for the Autonomous Enterprise. Now comes the exciting part: turning that vision into practical outcomes for customers.

Join our Post-SAP Sapphire Briefing: SAP Business AI Platform – BUILD and explore how SAP Business AI is moving from concept to execution.

In this session, we’ll revisit the platform vision and then take a closer look at Joule Studio, where partners can build intelligent, business-aware agents designed to support real business processes. We’ll also explore SAP Integration Suite and how connected systems help create seamless end-to-end experiences across the enterprise.

Think of it as connecting the dots between AI innovation and day-to-day business value.

We’ll wrap up with a live Q&A, giving you the opportunity to ask questions, share perspectives, and learn from fellow partners.

Register now and discover how these capabilities fit into SAP’s AI-driven ecosystem.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/jun/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014331.html

entry.title: Re: CBC Mandatory SD Organizational Units Due to Baseline...
entry.content:
Hi @edoymaner 

As you mentioned the SD processes are part of the minimum basic scope bundle that must be selected in the scoping setup

The organizational entities you create are also determined by the country selection made in the previous step.

It’s important to note that some entities are mandatory, as they serve as the fundamental building blocks for all business processes. Without them, the selected scope items cannot function properly.
The Set Up Organizational Structure app is also closely linked to the scope definition, meaning the selected scope determines which organizational units are required for the underlying processes (see Organizational Structure Management | SAP Help Portal).

You must create all mandatory org units (those marked under “Units to be created”) before confirming them. This step is essential to confirm the milestone and move to the next phase of your configuration.

As outlined in 3165028 - Mandatory organizational units to be created and confirm button in SAP Central Business Co..., these entities must be created to ensure the consistency of your organizational structure.

If certain organizational units are not relevant for your system, you can create them using placeholder names such as “DO NOT USE” for both the name and description.
When the system indicates mandatory organizational units, there is no workaround to skip their creation. If you plan to keep your current scope, it will be necessary to complete this step to maintain consistency and proceed with further activities.

For additional details, please also review 3537843 - Frequently asked questions in the organization management area in SAP Central Business Con...

Regards,

Lucian

entry.title: Re: Run Datasphere Task Chains from SAC Multi-Actions via External API Step
entry.content:
@manu_xu 

hello,

the issue is in the interaction between SAC and Datasphere.

task chain with 1 parameter.

- the manual run of the task chain in datasphere UI Works.

- the manual run of the task chain via Datasphere CLI works.

- the manual run of the task chain via REST API (postman) works.

- the run via SAC multiaction does not work by specifying the input parameter in the body of the request or in the header or in the URL. it worked only without specifying the input parameter and it was executed with default value.

when adding the input parameter the task chain was hanging for 40+ minutes without starting the first task.
when started manually from datasphere UI the task chain completed in 1 minute (with ot without passing input parameter).

Best regards,
Alberto

entry.title: QC21 without usage decision
entry.content:
Dear experts,

Is there any configuration available to make the QC21 (Quality Certificate for Inspection Lot) working also for Insp.lots without the Usage Decision ?

Thank you in advance.

KR

Massimo

entry.title: Object Dependency assigned Routing Operation 0162 Not Picked During CO01 Order Creation
entry.content:
We are facing an issue during production order creation (CO01) where, operation 0162 is not selected in the routing although the operation has a selection condition assigned that should evaluate to true for the affected material.

The issue occurs for configurable materials that use the same configurable material, routing group, routing counter, production plant, planning plant, and order type.

The only relevant difference between the materials is the value of characteristic Z_VC_VORWERTGRUPPE.

Master Data:

  1. Order Type: ZP01
  2. Production Plant: 7010
  3. Planning Plant: 7001
  4. Materials:
    • WNPW060331K6BEES
    • WNPW060310K0BEES

      5. Configurable Materia same for both materials:

    • VCR_TNPW0603_EWU

       6. Routing: same routing group and counter for both the materials. It's maintained for production plant 7010.

        7. O

entry.title: Re: multi sub report in multi section detail
entry.content:
Hello


In each subreport section (Details b/c):
Right-click section → Section Expert

Uncheck: New Page Before / New Page After
Uncheck: Keep Together

For each subreport object:
Right-click → Format Subreport

Uncheck: Keep Object Together
Enable: Can Grow

This allows subreport 2 to continue in the remaining space instead of jumping to a new page.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Portfolio Hierarchy creation Capabilities in SAP EPPM Public cloud
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are currently using SAP Portfolio and Project Management (PPM) in an on-premise landscape, where we actively leverage functionalities such as:

  • Portfolio creation and structuring
  • Bucket creation for categorization and prioritization
  • Portfolio Item creation and linking it to SAP PS for execution.

As part of our roadmap, we are now planning a transition to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and would like to understand the availability and extent of these capabilities in the cloud environment.

Specifically, we are looking for guidance on the following:

  1. Whether Portfolio, Bucket, and Item creation functionalities are available in SAP Public Cloud (and under which scope ID/Item)
  2. Any insights, documentation references, or experience-based recommendations would be highly appreciated.

Thanks in advance for your support!

entry.title: Manage Fixed Asset – Request to Reinstate Description Length Validation into new version
entry.content:
We noticed that in the new version of Manage Fixed Asset, the system allows users to enter an asset description exceeding the 50-character limit without any restriction. This is inconvenient for users, as they are only informed of the issue when attempting to proceed and must then manually shorten the description.

We would like to seek your support in restoring the behavior in the old version, whereby the system would either prevent further input or automatically truncate the description once the 50-character limit is reached.

Could you please advise whether this functionality can be reinstated?

entry.title: Re: Valuation type differs from valuation type of batch
entry.content:
• If the PO valuation type is wrong → Amend PO before GR.

  • If the batch valuation type is wrong → Correct batch master data.

  • If both are correct but still mismatched → Use a batch that matches the PO valuation type.

entry.title: Primary Blog - SAP Cloud ALM for Operations
entry.content:
Welcome to Your One-Stop Hub for SAP Cloud ALM for Operations Blogs

If you've ever found yourself searching across multiple pages, bookmarks, or community threads to track down the right SAP Cloud ALM for Operations content — this blog is for you.

SAP Cloud ALM for Operations is a rapidly evolving solution, and with that growth comes a wealth of knowledge scattered across blog posts, how-to guides, release highlights, and expert insights published by the SAP community. While each of those posts is valuable on its own, finding them at the right moment can be a challenge.

That's exactly why this blog exists.

This post serves as a living index — a single, curated destination where you can find links to all relevant SAP Cloud ALM for Operations blogs in one place. Whether you're just getting started with the solution or you're a seasoned administrator looking for a specific topic, this collection is designed to save you time and help you get to the right content faster.

The list will be continuously updated as new blogs are published, so feel free to bookmark this page and return whenever you need a reliable starting point for your SAP Cloud ALM for Operations journey.

Let's make finding the right knowledge a little easier — together.

Please note: Recently published blogs are indicated with a New tag.

Never miss an update — find the Subscribe button at the top-right and click it.

  

SAP Cloud ALM for Operations Overview:

  • SAP Adoption Labs: SAP Cloud ALM for Operations: Learn the business value and Step by Step on how to implement
  • Operating Cloud-Centric Solutions with SAP Cloud ALM - openSAP
  • SAP Cloud ALM for Operations – Overview

SAP Cloud ALM - General

  • More insights, faster resolution: Expanding Base Memory for SAP Cloud ALM
  • SAP Cloud ALM Memory Estimator

Business Process Monitoring

  • Four Steps to Get Started with Business Process Monitoring Powered by SAP Cloud ALM
  • SAP Cloud ALM - Business Process Monitoring
  • SAP Cloud for Customer KPI Now Available
  • Tailoring Business Process Monitoring for your Business Users
  • How to Count Table Entries with a Custom KPI

Integration and Exception Monitoring

  • Monitoring End-to-End Master Data Replication using SAP Cloud ALM
  • Cloud ALM - Solving Tips for Detected Exceptions Raised for your Supported Solutions
  • End-to-end Monitoring of an Integration Scenario
  • Analyze Exception Logs of your Landscape, using SAP Cloud ALM Exception Monitoring
  • Monitoring Event Mesh from SAP Integration Suite with SAP Cloud ALM

Real User Monitoring and Synthetic User Monitoring

  • Synthetic User Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM: How to Configure an On-Premise Runner
  • How to Create a Simple SUM Scenario for the Fiori Launchpad
  • Let’s Start your Synthetic User Monitoring Journey
  • AI-Powered Operational Excellence in Cloud ALM’s Real User Monitoring

Configuration and Security Analysis

  • Exploring Configuration and Security Analysis in SAP Cloud ALM – IT Admin perspective
  • Introducing Configuration and Security Analysis – Validation application in SAP Cloud ALM

Health Monitoring 

  • Cloud ALM Health Monitoring for Event Configuration
  • Introducing Custom Events in SAP Cloud ALM Health Monitoring

Business Service Management 

  • Unified Overview of Status Events for Business Services

SAP Cloud ALM APIs

  • SAP ALM API & Integration: Updates
  • SAP ALM API & Integration: Events to External Chat Systems
  • SAP ALM API & Integration: Status Events to Incidents
  • Integrate SAP Cloud ALM with External Systems
  • SAP Cloud ALM APIs: What’s new in 2025?

Open Telemetry

  • SAP Cloud ALM: Next Generation - Data Collection Infrastructure
  • Observability for Third Party Components using SAP Cloud ALM
  • Instrumenting SAP NetWeaver Java AS with OpenTelemetry for Monitoring with SAP Cloud ALM

For further Information refer to Expert Portal.

Got ideas? We want to hear them! Comment below with what you'd like us to add, improve, or explore next.

entry.title: Re: Opening Content Server Documents On the Internet
entry.content:
Hello @bala_kanaya 

Content Server returns its internal URL, causing hostname unknown externally.

Configure Content Server base URL / external URL mapping to use the WAF/public URL.
Enable reverse proxy for Content Server and route calls through it.
Maintain correct host mapping so URLs are rewritten externally.

Access Content Server only via Web Dispatcher/WAF with URL rewriting.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Error Function code not defined for flow EWM RF Custom Application
entry.content:
Hello all,

I am creating a custom RF application in SAP S/4HANA and I’m running into an issue where the screen is not rendering properly. I found a YouTube video suggesting this may happen when the PBO and PAI function modules have not been implemented yet, and in my case, that is true.

Could someone please explain the correct way to build the PBO and PAI function modules for a custom RF screen? I’m especially looking for guidance on:

  • How to structure the INIT/PBO and user input/PAI modules.

  • How to pass data to the RF screen correctly.

  • Which RF customizing entries must be maintained so the screen renders and the flow continues properly.

From what I’ve learned, SAP EWM RF screens require PBO and PAI function modules for each screen step, and the corresponding FM must be assigned in the RF step flow and screen mapping. I would appreciate any sample logic or a step-by-step explanation for a basic custom RF transaction.

entry.title: Re: Poland: Invoice Number
entry.content: The control is in the ODN framework for Poland localization, not in the standard billing document number range SSCUI. Service material invoices are deliberately mapped to a separate ODN type, which enforces the 183******* range.

entry.title: Crystal Reports Runtime 13.0.35 / VB.NET issue with subreport logons
entry.content:
Crystal Reports Runtime 13.0.35 / VB.NET issue with subreport logons

I have a VB.NET report runner using Crystal Reports Runtime 13.0.35.

The runner dynamically changes reports from the design server:

DESKTOP-94TBRUD\MSSQLSERVER01

to the runtime server:

BRPSQL1

using RAS ReplaceConnection() and additional DataSourceConnection.SetConnection() calls.

Facts established:

1. Main report connection successfully changes to BRPSQL1.

2. Embedded subreport "Boning Room Input.rpt" contains 3 SQL tables:

* rptYieldCarcaseDetails
* ProductMaster
* ProductDescriptions

3. Runtime logging confirms:

* subDoc.DataSourceConnections(0).SetConnection(BRPSQL1, Meat_Plant, ...)
* DataSourceConnections(0).ServerName = BRPSQL1
* DataSourceConnections(0).DatabaseName = Meat_Plant

4. Runtime logging also confirms all subreport tables are updated:

VERIFY SERVER = BRPSQL1
VERIFY DATABASE = Meat_Plant

for all three tables.

5. Crystal RAS reports:

OLD CONNECTION COUNT = 1

6. Despite all logged connections showing BRPSQL1, the viewer still behaves as follows:

* First login prompt = BRPSQL1
* Credentials entered
* Second login prompt = DESKTOP-94TBRUD\MSSQLSERVER01
* Logon Failed

7. If the Boning Room Input subreport is removed from the main report, the second login prompt disappears.

8. The subreport is NOT an on-demand subreport.

9. Database Expert shows standard SQL tables under:
DESKTOP-94TBRUD\MSSQLSERVER01

Question:

Has anyone seen a Crystal subreport continue prompting for the original design-time server after:

* ReplaceConnection()
* DataSourceConnections.SetConnection()
* Table ApplyLogOnInfo()

all report success and all table/server verification logging shows the new server?

What other Crystal objects can retain a saved datasource/login independently of the tables and DataSourceConnections collection?

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 05 - Portable functions
Implementing RAP Action Parameter Value Help Using CDS Abstract Entity in SAP RAP
entry.content:
Introduction

The ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a powerful way to build modern SAP Fiori applications using behavior definitions, CDS views, and OData services.

One common requirement in business applications is allowing users to execute actions on one or multiple records while providing additional input through a dialog popup. In many scenarios, this input should be selected from a predefined list of values rather than entered manually.

A typical example is updating the category of one or more products. Users should be able to trigger an action, choose a valid category from a value help dialog, and apply the selected category to all chosen records.

This blog demonstrates how to implement an RAP Action Parameter with Value Help using a CDS Abstract Entity, CDS Value Help View, and Behavior Action. The result is a Fiori Elements application where users can execute an action and select values through standard SAP F4 help.

What are RAP Action Parameters?

Actions in RAP allow developers to execute custom business logic that goes beyond standard Create, Update, and Delete operations.

An action can:

  • Execute custom business logic
  • Update one or multiple records
  • Trigger validations
  • Open parameter dialogs for user input

When an action requires user input, RAP automatically generates a dialog popup using an Abstract Entity.

This makes actions ideal for scenarios such as:

  • Approving business documents
  • Assigning categories
  • Updating statuses
  • Triggering business processes

Real-Time Business Scenario: Mass Category Assignment

Consider a product management application.

Business users maintain products and their categories.

Examples:

Product ID Product Name Category

P1001LaptopELEC
P1002Office ChairFURN
P1003T-ShirtCLTH

A user selects multiple products and wants to update their category simultaneously.

Instead of editing each product individually:

  1. Select one or more products
  2. Click Set Category
  3. Choose a category from F4 Help
  4. Confirm the action

All selected products are updated automatically.

The Problem

If the category field is entered manually:

  • Users may enter invalid values
  • Validation becomes difficult
  • User experience is poor
  • Data consistency cannot be guaranteed

Example:

ELEC  ✔ Valid
FURN  ✔ Valid
ABCD  ✖ Invalid
XYZ1  ✖ Invalid

Without a value help mechanism, users can easily enter incorrect data.

The Solution

To solve this problem, we implement:

  • A Category Master table
  • A CDS Value Help View
  • A CDS Abstract Entity for action parameters
  • A RAP Action with parameter support
  • A Fiori Elements Value Help dialog

The action parameter field is linked to the value help CDS entity using:

@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition

This automatically generates:

  • F4 Help
  • Search dialog
  • Value validation
  • User-friendly selection experience

Implementation Using RAP Action Parameter Value Help

Step 1: Create Category Master Table

Create a database table to store available product categories.

@EndUserText.label : 'Product Category Table'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zprod_category {

  key client      : abap.clnt not null;
  key category_id : abap.char(4) not null;
  category_name   : abap.char(40);

}

This table acts as the master source for the value help.

 

Step 2: Create Product Table

Create a product table containing:

 

  • Product ID
  • Product Name
  • Category
  • Audit fields

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Product Table'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zproduct_s {

  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key product_id        : abap.char(10) not null;
  product_name          : abap.char(40);
  category              : abap.char(4);
  created_by            : abp_creation_user;
  created_at            : abp_creation_tstmpl;
  last_changed_by       : abp_locinst_lastchange_user;
  last_changed_at       : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;

}

This table stores the transactional product data.

 

Step 3: Create Value Help CDS View

Create a CDS View Entity that exposes category information.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Category Value Help'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}

define view entity ZI_CategoryVH
  as select from zprod_category
{
  key category_id   as CategoryId,
      category_name as CategoryName
}

Explanation:

  • Provides the source for value help
  • Supplies category IDs and descriptions
  • Used by both RAP and Fiori Elements

Step 4: Create Root Interface View

Create the RAP Root Interface View.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product Interface View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZI_PRODUCT_S as select from zproduct_s
{
    
  key product_id              as ProductId,
      product_name            as ProductName,
      category                as Category,

      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      created_by              as CreatedBy,

      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      created_at              as CreatedAt,

      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      last_changed_by         as LastChangedBy,

      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      last_changed_at         as LastChangedAt,

      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      local_last_changed_at   as LocalLastChangedAt
}

This view represents the Product business object.

 

Step 5: Create Projection View

Create the Projection View exposed to Fiori.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product Projection View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define root view entity ZC_PRODUCT
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on ZI_PRODUCT_S
{
  key ProductId,
      ProductName,
      @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
         entity: {
           name: 'ZI_CATEGORYVH',
           element: 'CategoryId'
         }
       }]
      Category,
      CreatedBy,
      CreatedAt,
      LastChangedBy,
      LastChangedAt,
      LocalLastChangedAt
}

This enables F4 Help on the Category field.

Step 6: Create Abstract Entity for Action Parameters

The action dialog parameters are defined using a CDS Abstract Entity.

@EndUserText.label: 'Parameter for Set Category Action'
define abstract entity ZA_SETCATEGORY_PARAM
{
  @EndUserText.label: 'New Category'
  @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
    entity    : {
      name    : 'ZI_CATEGORYVH',
      element : 'CategoryId'
    },
    useForValidation: true
  }]
  NewCategory : abap.char(4);

}

Explanation:

  • Generates the popup field
  • Provides F4 help
  • Enables automatic validation

Important:

useForValidation: true

prevents invalid values from being entered.

 

Step 7: Create Interface Behavior Definition

Define the RAP Action.

managed implementation in class zbp_i_product_s unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_PRODUCT_S alias Product
persistent table zproduct_s
lock master
authorization master ( instance )
etag master LastChangedAt
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;

  field ( readonly ) ProductId, CreatedBy, CreatedAt,
  LastChangedBy, LastChangedAt,
  LocalLastChangedAt;

  field ( mandatory ) ProductName;

  action setCategory parameter ZA_SETCATEGORY_PARAM
    result [1] $self;

  mapping for zproduct_s corresponding
    {
      ProductId          = product_id;
      ProductName        = product_name;
      Category           = category;
      CreatedBy          = created_by;
      CreatedAt          = created_at;
      LastChangedBy      = last_changed_by;
      LastChangedAt      = last_changed_at;
      LocalLastChangedAt = local_last_changed_at;
    }
}

 

Step 8: Implement Action Logic

In the behavior implementation class:

METHOD setcategory.

The implementation:

  1. Reads selected products
  2. Retrieves action parameter value
  3. Updates category field
  4. Returns updated records

The selected category is obtained from:

keys[ ... ]-%param-NewCategory
CLASS lhc_Product DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR Product RESULT result.

    METHODS setCategory FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION Product~setCategory RESULT result.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_Product IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get_instance_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD setCategory.

    " Read current product records
    READ ENTITIES OF zi_product_s IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Product
        FIELDS ( ProductId Category )
        WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(lt_products).

    " Update category field for each selected product
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zi_product_s IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Product
        UPDATE FIELDS ( Category )
        WITH VALUE #(
          FOR ls_prod IN lt_products
          (
            %tky     = ls_prod-%tky
            Category = keys[ KEY entity
                              %key = ls_prod-%key
                            ]-%param-NewCategory
          )
        )
      REPORTED DATA(lt_reported).

    " Return updated records as action result
    READ ENTITIES OF zi_product_s IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Product
        ALL FIELDS
        WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(lt_result).

    result = VALUE #(
      FOR ls_res IN lt_result
      ( %tky   = ls_res-%tky
        %param = ls_res )
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Step 9: Create Projection Behavior Definition

Expose the action:

projection;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZC_PRODUCT //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;

  use action setCategory;
}

 

Step 10: Create Metadata Extension

Add UI annotations.

@Metadata.layer: #CORE

annotate entity ZC_PRODUCT with
{
  @ui.facet: [
  {
    id:       'ProductInfo',
    type:     #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
    label:    'Product Information',
    position: 10
  }
  ]

  @ui.lineItem: [
    { position: 10, label: 'Product ID' },
    { type: #FOR_ACTION, dataAction: 'setCategory',
      label: 'Set Category', position: 40 }
  ]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 10 }]
  @ui.selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
  ProductId;

  @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Product Name' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 20 }]
  ProductName;

  @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Category' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 30 }]
  Category;

}

 

Step 11: Create Service Definition

Expose both entities.

@EndUserText.label: 'Product Service'
define service ZSD_PRODUCT {
  expose ZC_PRODUCT    as Product;
  expose ZI_CategoryVH as CategoryVH;
}

Important:

Many developers forget to expose the value help entity.

Without exposing:

ZI_CATEGORY_VH

the F4 dialog will not display any data.

 

Step 12: Create Service Binding

Create:

OData V4 - UI

service binding.

Activate and Publish the service.

 

Result

Once the application is launched, users can:

1. Select Products

☑ P1001 Laptop
☑ P1002 Office Chair

2. Click Set Category

[ Set Category ]

3. Action Dialog Opens

Set Category

New Category [      ] 🔍

4. Open Value Help

ELEC  Electronics
FURN  Furniture
CLTH  Clothing
FOOD  Food & Beverages

5. Choose a Category

Example:

ELEC

6. Confirm

OK

7. RAP Action Executes

Selected records are updated automatically.

8. List Refreshes

P1001  Laptop        ELEC
P1002  Office Chair  ELEC

Success message:

Category updated successfully

 

Conclusion

In this blog, we explored how to implement RAP Action Parameters with Value Help using a CDS Abstract Entity.

We discussed a real-world business scenario where users need to update product categories for multiple records while ensuring only valid values can be selected.

By combining a CDS Value Help View, Abstract Entity, RAP Action, and Fiori Elements annotations, we were able to create a user-friendly action dialog with built-in F4 help and validation.

This approach provides a reusable pattern for implementing parameterized actions in RAP and can easily be extended for approval processes, status updates, assignment actions, and many other business scenarios while maintaining a clean and scalable design.

entry.title: ENHANCEMNET ON BP SCREEN USING BDT ANALYZER
entry.content:
ENHANCEMNET ON BP SCREEN USING BDT ANALYZER

BDT (Business Data Toolset) in SAP is a powerful, centralized framework that controls the maintenance, display, and enhancement of master data (like the Business Partner) and simple transactional data. It allows developers to add custom fields, create new tabs, and implement validations without modifying standard SAP code. This document will help you in enhancing Business Partner using BDT (Business Data Toolset).

This technical guide provides a step-by-step walkthrough on how to seamlessly enhance the Business Partner master data screen by adding a custom field under the Identification tab. Utilizing the power of the Business Data Toolset (BDT)—the standard, modular framework provided by SAP for customizing dialog screens—we will explore how to configure backend tables, design custom subscreen layouts, handle screen flow logic, and seamlessly map new data elements without disrupting standard SAP functionalities.

Steps to add a custom field in BP under Identification Tab:

1. Go to SE11, create a include structure or append structure in the table where you want to save your additional custom fields.

In my case I want to add my custom fields to BUT000 table. So, I created a global structure and append it in the BUT000 table as shown below.   

 

2. Go to Tcode - BUPT->Business Partner->Control->Screen Layout->Field Groups (BUS2)

3. Go to Edit and click on New Entries, a new window will pop up. Mention all the details below. Custom Field Group range can be 600-699.

4. Now select the created Field Group and click on Field Groups->Fields.

Enter the table name and global structure along with field as below and mark global structure as input field.

5. Go to Tcode- SE80 and create a Function Group, declare global variable in Top include.

 Create a new screen with the details below:

Click on layout and create a custom field along with text add Ip/op field.                                                 

Save and activate the layout.

6. Go to flow logic: Create Module by double click on it.

In PBO, add logic for your custom field.

Similarly, add your logic in PAI.

7. BUPT->Business Partner->Control->Screen Layout->Views (BUS3)

Click on New Entries and create a new custom view along with other details as below:

Assign your program name and screen number from the function group.

then double click on View->Field Group assign the field group created earlier in BUS2.

8. BUPT->Business Partner->Control->Screen Layout->Sections (BUS3)

Click on new entries and create a new section assigning the same view created earlier in BUS3.

9. BUPT->Business Partner->Control->Screen Layout->Screens (BUS4)                                                     

Click on new Entries and create a new screen, enter description and screen title which you want for your screen.

Selecting the entry, double click on Screen->Sections and assign the section created.

Here we have added Header Data (BUP009) section as we are going to use the header data section of the standard screen along with a newly created section.

10. BUPT->Business Partner->Control->Screen Sequence (BUS6)

Create a new entry and earlier created screen as below:

Create a Screen Sequence Category and assign the Screen Sequence.

11. Now go to Tcode - BP.

Click on Person, Organization or Group, then Identification tab.

We can see the Custom field which we have created is appearing as expected.

By leveraging the Business Data Toolset (BDT) analyzer, we have successfully extended the standard SAP Business Partner functionality to include a custom user-defined field within the Identification tab. This process highlights the modular flexibility of SAP BDT, demonstrating how data dictionary enhancements (BUT000), custom flow logic (PBO/PAI), and custom screen configurations can be bound together dynamically without modifying core SAP source code.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Delete Payment Method P for Company Code 1004
entry.content:
Hi jongbin,

The error message Deletion not possible for record created by SAP strictly appears when a configuration record is part of the pre-delivered SAP Best Practices content. I am deducing here that Company Code 1004 in your system is (or was originally deployed as) a standard SAP template company code, and the assignment of Payment Method P is standard content for it. The configuration framework intentionally blocks the deletion of these pre-delivered records to protect underlying system integrity and prevent update disruptions. You were likely able to delete the payment method for other company codes because those assignments were custom-created rather than pre-delivered by SAP out-of-the-box.

This standard restriction is explicitly documented in SAP Note 3595270.

Solution / Workaround: Instead of trying to physically delete the record, you should effectively "disable" it to prevent any usage:

  1. Rely on your removed dependent configuration:  Make sure that for CC 1004, payment method P has no entry at all in activity Set Up Bank Determination for Payment Transactions - i.e. no Ranking Order row, no Bank Accounts row, and no Available Amounts row for P across any of CC 1004's house banks/currencies. The Manage Automatic Payment app will error out and bypass Payment Method P automatically if anyone attempts to use it.

  2. Restrict via Amount Limits: In the configuration activity Set Up Payment Methods per Company Code for Payment Transactions, select PM P for CC 1004 and set the Maximum Amount limit to 0,01. While this can be bypassed manually in the payment program, it practically means the method cannot be automatically used for payment runs.

  3. Master Data Review: Ensure Payment Method P is not assigned to any Business Partner (Supplier/Employee) master data under CC 1004.

If there is a strict compliance requirement to physically wipe this record from the database, you must open a CASE with SAP Support and request an Expert Configuration (they might require you to raise WCR), though SAP typically advises using the limitation workarounds above for standard payment methods.

Hope it helps,

Michal

entry.title: Re: No one can log in to CALM
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Greetings from SAP Support.

Please refer to below KBA:

3365171 - How to get full Admin/IT Contact access for SAP Cloud ALM? - SAP for Me 

For your scenario, ehe existing SAP Cloud ALM Administrator is not available, global account and Subaccount Administrator assignment is now self service

Admin access for BTP cockpit SAP Cloud ALM Global and Subaccounts

Refer to these KBAs for more information:

  • KBA 2669325 – How to add a user as a Global Account Administrator for SAP BTP
  • KBA 3412209 – How to assign yourself to a Subaccount as a Global Account Admin

Admin access for SAP Cloud ALM tenant

  1. Perform these steps only after completing section 2.2.1. "Admin access for BTP cockpit SAP Cloud ALM Global and Subaccounts"
  2. Log on to the SAP BTP cockpit.
  3. Open the SAP Cloud ALM Global Account.
  4. Open the SAP Cloud ALM Subaccount.
  5. Select the Security menu on the left of the screen.
  6. Select the Users tab under Security.
  7. Locate your user entry for the Custom IAS tenant.
  8. Assign the Cross Global Administrator role collection.
  9. Save.

Admin access for the IAS tenant

  1. Check whether an existing IAS Administrator is available as described in KBA 3035908.
  2. If none can be reached, add an IAS Administrator via Self-Service in the IAS Administrators Card.
  3. If further assistance is required, open a case with BC-IAM-IDS.

Please let me know if you need further assistance.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: CAP Now Supports ReactJS - With working demo + UI5 Web components
entry.content:
Based on the latest CAP update in the official docs, React and VueJS are now fully supported as frontend options. I tested the setup end‑to‑end based on the official docs link and prepared a working demo in BTP Trial.

Reference links:

  • CAP-React-Vue Example Project 
  • UI5 Web Components for React - Docs 

Anyhow, to start with, the blog shall include:

  1. Creating Bookshop CAP and adding React app
  2. Installing UI5 Web for React dependencies
  3. Local testing
  4. Setting up IAS and HANA deployment

Pre requisites:

  • @SAP/cds-dk library should be at least 9^ or latest version. 
  • CAP version should be latest
  • Your BTP Subaccount is subscribed to SAP HANA Cloud with instance
  • Your BTP Subaccount is subscribed to CIS and Application Frontend Service with instances

To ensure latest CAP version, use BAS

1. Create Bookshop CAP and adding React app

  • Log-in to your BAS or VS-code
  • Open the terminal and enter the command to initiate CAP sample project 

  • initiate adding react application using this command cds add react --into <your-react-app>

2. Installing UI5 Web Components for React dependencies

  • Open the terminal, go to your app folder
  • As per the official doc, enter the following commands:

  • Utilize UI5 elements. (See my example below):

App.jsx

import { Bar, Card, Label, Page, Table, TableCell, TableHeaderCell, TableHeaderRow, TableRow, Title } from '@ui5/webcomponents-react'
import { useEffect, useState } from 'react'

export default function App() {
  const [books, setBooks] = useState([])

  useEffect(() => {
    fetch('/odata/v4/catalog/Books')
      .then(r => r.json())
      .then(r => setBooks(r.value))
  }, [])

  return (
    <>
      <Page backgroundDesign='Solid'
        header={<Bar design='Header'>
           <Title>Books</Title>
        </Bar>}
        style={{ width: '100vw', height: '100vh' }}
      >
        <Card style={{ width: '150px', height: '150px', marginBottom: '3rem', position: 'relative', marginTop: '3rem' }}>
          <Label style={{ padding: '1rem'}}>Total Book count:</Label>
            
            <Title size='H1' style={{ padding: '1rem', position: 'absolute', bottom: '1%', textAlign: 'right' }}>{books.length}</Title>
        </Card>
        <Table headerRow={<TableHeaderRow sticky>
                            <TableHeaderCell>Title</TableHeaderCell>
                            <TableHeaderCell>Author</TableHeaderCell>
                          </TableHeaderRow>}>
           {books.map(b=>(
            <TableRow key={b.ID}>
             <TableCell key={b.ID}>
               <span>{b.title}</span>
             </TableCell>
             <TableCell>
                <span><i> by {b.author}</i></span>
             </TableCell>
            </TableRow>
           ))}
        </Table>
      </Page>
    </>
  )
}

3. Local Testing

  • Run the app (cds watch)

4. Setting up IAS and HANA Deployment

As per official document, you just need the following commands for deployment

  • cds add app-frontend
  • cds add ias  or  cds add xsuaa
  • cds add hana
  • cds up

cds up will automatically create the .mta file and deploy the CAP, React App, IAS and HANA deployer instances

Conclusion:

With all these pieces in place, you now have a full CAP + React setup running end‑to‑end, from local development to IAS authentication and HANA deployment. This approach gives you the flexibility of a modern frontend while still taking advantage of CAP’s service layer, security model, and deployment tooling. Feel free to extend the sample, plug in your own UI components, or adapt the structure for your next project.

Let me know if you have any further questions or wish to communicate, just comment below.
